blocks.content: In the previous lesson we built the open ball $B_r(p) = \{\, x : d(x,p) < r \,\}$ — the set of points within distance $r$ of $p$. Balls are the lens through which all of topology is seen. We now use them to classify sets by how their points sit inside.

Call $p$ an **interior point** of a set $E$ if some ball around $p$ stays entirely inside $E$ — that is, there is a radius $r>0$ with $B_r(p) \subseteq E$. Intuitively, $p$ has a little breathing room: a whole neighborhood of $p$ is still in $E$.
A set $E$ is **open** if *every* one of its points is an interior point. No point of an open set sits on its edge; each one has room to spare.
The partner notion is defined by complements. A set $E$ is **closed** if its complement $E^c$ is open.

blocks.latex: E \text{ is closed} \iff E^c \text{ is open}.

blocks.content: Beware a common trap: open and closed are **not** opposites. A set can be both — $\mathbb{R}$ and $\varnothing$ are each open and closed. And a set can be neither: the half-open interval $[a,b)$ is the classic example, as we will see.

steps.prompt: Look at the interval $(-1,1)$ and its two endpoints. Pick any point strictly inside, say $0.6$: you can draw a tiny ball around it that stays inside $(-1,1)$, so it is an interior point. But $\pm 1$ are *not* in $(-1,1)$, and any ball around an endpoint would spill past it. Every interior point having room is exactly what 'open' means.
Let us prove the open interval $(a,b)$ really is an open set.

blocks.content: Take any $p \in (a,b)$, so $a < p < b$. To show $p$ is an interior point we must produce a radius $r>0$ with $(p-r,\,p+r) \subseteq (a,b)$.
Since $a<p<b$ we have $p-a>0$ and $b-p>0$, so this $r$ is strictly positive.

blocks.latex: p-r \ge p-(p-a) = a \qquad\text{and}\qquad p+r \le p+(b-p) = b.

blocks.content: Therefore $(p-r,\,p+r) \subseteq (a,b)$, so $p$ is an interior point. Because $p$ was arbitrary, every point of $(a,b)$ is interior, and $(a,b)$ is open. $\blacksquare$

steps.prompt: Take $a<b$. Among these subsets of $\mathbb{R}$, which one is **neither open nor closed**?

feedback.correct: Right. $[a,b)$ is not open because the endpoint $a$ belongs to it but every ball around $a$ contains points $<a$ that are outside. It is not closed either, because its complement contains $b$, and no ball around $b$ avoids the set (points just below $b$ are in $[a,b)$). So it is neither.

incorrect.message: $(a,b)$ is open: we just proved every point of it is interior.
$[a,b]$ is closed: its complement $(-\infty,a)\cup(b,\infty)$ is a union of open intervals, hence open.
$\mathbb{R}$ is open (every ball stays inside) and also closed (its complement $\varnothing$ is open). It is the wrong kind of example here.
Look for the half-open interval. It includes one endpoint (so it is not open there) and excludes the other (so its complement is not open there either).

steps.prompt: Here is a *flawed* argument that the intersection $\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} G_n$ of the open sets $G_n=\left(-\tfrac1n,\tfrac1n\right)$ is open. Exactly one step is invalid — select it.

steps.label: Each $G_n=\left(-\tfrac1n,\tfrac1n\right)$ is an open interval, hence open, and $0\in G_n$ for every $n$.
For each $n$, the ball of radius $r_n=\tfrac1n$ about $0$ satisfies $\left(-r_n,r_n\right)\subseteq G_n$.
Let $r=\min_{n} r_n$; then $r>0$ and $(-r,r)\subseteq G_n$ for every $n$, so $(-r,r)\subseteq\bigcap_n G_n$.
Hence $0$ is an interior point of the intersection, so $\bigcap_n G_n$ is open.

feedback.correct: Exactly — step 3 is the flaw. The numbers $r_n=1/n$ have infimum $0$, and there is no *smallest* one, so $\min_n r_n$ does not exist as a positive number; the would-be minimum is $0$. With no positive $r$ working for all $n$, the argument collapses. In fact $\bigcap_n G_n=\{0\}$, which is **not** open — confirming infinite intersections of open sets can fail to be open.

incorrect.message: Step 1 is fine: each $(-1/n,1/n)$ is a genuine open interval and clearly contains $0$.
Step 2 is correct: the ball of radius $1/n$ about $0$ is exactly $G_n$, so it certainly sits inside $G_n$.
Step 4 follows validly *if* the previous step held — the error is upstream, in the claim that a positive $r$ works for all $n$ at once.
Ask which step quietly assumes infinitely many positive numbers have a positive minimum. The radii $1/n$ shrink to $0$, so no single positive $r$ fits inside every $G_n$.

steps.prompt: Which statement about open sets in a metric space is **true**?

choices.label: The union of *any* collection of open sets is open, and the intersection of *finitely many* open sets is open.
The intersection of *any* collection of open sets is open.
The union of two open sets is open, but the union of infinitely many open sets is never open.

feedback.correct: Correct. Arbitrary unions of open sets are open: any point of the union lies in some member, and that member supplies a ball. Finite intersections stay open because you can take the *minimum* of finitely many positive radii. Infinitude is exactly where the second part must stop.

incorrect.message: Infinite intersections can fail: $\bigcap_n(-1/n,1/n)=\{0\}$ is not open. The min-of-radii trick needs *finitely* many sets.
Arbitrary unions of open sets *are* open — even infinitely many. For instance $\bigcup_n(-1+1/n,\,1-1/n)=(-1,1)$. Unions are the unrestricted operation.
Unions: unrestricted. Intersections: only *finitely* many are guaranteed to stay open.

blocks.content: A set is **open** when every point is interior — each point sits inside some ball contained in the set. A set is **closed** when its complement is open. These are not opposites: $\mathbb{R}$ and $\varnothing$ are both, while $[a,b)$ is neither.
Standard examples: $(a,b)$ is open, $[a,b]$ is closed, and a half-open interval is neither.

The operation rules are asymmetric. **Arbitrary** unions of open sets are open, and **finite** intersections of open sets are open — but infinite intersections may fail, the witness being $\bigcap_n(-1/n,1/n)=\{0\}$. By taking complements, arbitrary intersections of closed sets are closed and finite unions of closed sets are closed.

items.prompt: A set $E$ in a metric space is **open** when:

choices.label: For every $p\in E$ there is $r>0$ with $B_r(p)\subseteq E$.

feedback.correct: Yes — openness means every point is interior: each has a ball staying inside.

incorrect.message: Containing endpoints is closer to being *closed*; open sets in fact exclude their boundary points.
Openness has nothing to do with boundedness. $\mathbb{R}$ is open and unbounded; $\varnothing$ is open and empty.
Use the definition: every point must have *some* ball around it that lies entirely within the set.

items.prompt: Which is the correct definition of a **closed** set $E$?

feedback.correct: Right — closed is *defined* by its complement being open.

incorrect.message: Not-open and closed are different: $[a,b)$ is not open, yet it is also not closed. And $\mathbb{R}$ is both open and closed.
Finiteness is unrelated. $[0,1]$ is closed and infinite; $\{1/n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}$ is infinite and not closed.
Closedness is defined via complements: $E$ is closed exactly when $E^c$ is open.

choices.label: An infinite intersection of open sets can fail to be open: here it equals $\{0\}$, which is not open.
An infinite union of open sets can fail to be open.

feedback.correct: Exactly. Every $(-1/n,1/n)$ is open, but their intersection is the single point $\{0\}$, which has no ball inside it — so it is not open.

incorrect.message: This is an *intersection*, not a union — and arbitrary unions of open sets are always open. The lesson is that infinite *intersections* can fail.
The intersection is not empty: $0$ lies in every $(-1/n,1/n)$, so $\bigcap_n(-1/n,1/n)=\{0\}$.
Only $0$ survives every set, giving $\{0\}$ — a single point, which is not open. That shows infinite intersections need not be open.

feedback.correct: Yes — $(0,1)$ is open; every point has a ball staying inside.

incorrect.message: $[0,1]$ is closed, not open: no ball around the endpoint $0$ stays inside it.
$[0,1)$ is neither open nor closed: the endpoint $0$ has no ball staying inside.
An open interval excludes both endpoints, leaving every point with room around it.

retrievalBank.prompt: Arbitrary unions of open sets are ___, and finite intersections of open sets are ___.

feedback.correct: Correct — unions (any number) and finite intersections both preserve openness.

incorrect.message: These operations preserve *openness*, not closedness. It is intersections of *closed* sets that stay closed.
A finite intersection of open sets is open (take the minimum of finitely many radii), not closed.
Both blanks are 'open': arbitrary unions and finite intersections of open sets are open.
